mappings.rationaleAI: The Spanish Empire's discovery, exploration, and conquest of the Caribbean, Mexico, Central and South America (B.1), colonial administration via the Council of the Indies and viceroys (B.2.a), silver mining at Potosi and Zacatecas (B.2.c), Bourbon administrative reforms (B.4), and North American settlements and missions (B.5).
Columbus's four voyages (1492–1504) under Spanish sponsorship initiated European contact with the Americas, directly covering Spanish discovery and exploration of the Caribbean (B.1).

North America's geography, Paleo-Indian settlement, and European colonization (English, Spanish, French) connect to the geography and ethnography of the Americas (A), English colonies in North America (C), and French settlements (D).

Portuguese and Spanish maritime exploration, Columbus's voyages, and the Columbian Exchange directly cover Spanish discovery and exploration (B.1), while northern European exploration connects to Norse, English, and Dutch settlements (C.1) and French discoveries (D).

The forced transportation of Africans to the Americas connects to Indian policy including introduction of black slaves in Spanish colonies (B.2.b), Portuguese colonial introduction of black slavery in Brazil (B.3.a), and the introduction of slavery in English colonial society (C.3.b.iii).

South America's geography, pre-Columbian civilizations, and Spanish/Portuguese colonial history directly cover the geography and ethnography of the Americas (A) and Spanish and Portuguese colonization (B).

British colonial origins in Caribbean and North American colonies, rivalry with France and the Netherlands, and loss of American colonies (1783) connect to English territorial expansion into Spanish/Portuguese areas (B.6), English exploration and settlement (C.2), and development of the English colonies (C.3).

Mexico's pre-Columbian civilizations (Maya, Aztec) relate to the ethnography of the Americas (A), the Spanish conquest covers discovery and exploration (B.1), and three centuries of colonial rule connect to Spanish colonial expansion into North America (B.5).

Colonial grievances, taxation without representation, the Continental Army, and the Treaty of Paris (1783) directly cover the political and economic development of the English colonies (C.3.b) and the expulsion of French power culminating in American independence (C.3.d context).

The United States' origins in European colonization and the 1776 Revolution connect to the development of the English colonies in North America (C.3), including founding, economic development, and conflicts.
Colombia's Spanish colonization from 1499 and colonial-era governance connect to Spanish discovery, exploration, and conquest (B.1) and Spain's colonial empire administration (B.2).
Washington's French and Indian War service and leadership of the Continental Army connect to conflicts with the French and Indians and expulsion of French power from North America (C.3.d).
Norse voyages to Greenland and North America (c. 1000), including the Newfoundland settlement, directly cover Norse voyages to Greenland and North America (C.1).
Mesoamerica's Olmec, Maya, Aztec, and other civilizations directly relate to the ethnography of the Americas and pre-colonial indigenous cultures (A).
The Aztec Empire and Spanish conquest under Cortes in 1521 connect to the ethnography of the Americas (A) and Spanish discovery, exploration, and conquest of Mexico (B.1).
The Inca Empire's Andean civilization and the Spanish conquest beginning in 1532 connect to the ethnography of the Americas (A) and Spanish conquest of Peru (B.1).
Canada's history of French and British colonization connects to French discoveries, explorations, and settlements in North America and the settlement of New France (D.1).
European colonial domination through mercantilism and chartered companies directly relates to Spanish and Portuguese colonies (B), English colonies (C), and French settlements in North America (D).

France's colonial empire including New France connects to French discoveries, explorations, and settlements in North America (D), including missionaries, the fur trade, and royal administration (D.1).
Argentina's Spanish colonization connects to Spanish discovery, exploration, and conquest of Rio de la Plata (B.1).

Brazil's Portuguese colonization from 1500, introduction of slavery, gold and diamond mining, and centralized royal administration directly cover the exploration and colonization of Brazil (B.3, B.3.a, B.3.b).

Enlightenment political theory — constitutional government, natural rights — influenced colonial cultural and scientific achievements including the American Philosophical Society and the 'Great Awakening' (C.3.c).
The Rocky Mountains as North America's largest mountain system relate to the geography of the Americas (A).
The Reformation's fracturing of Western Christianity produced religious motivations for settlement in the founding of the 13 Colonies (C.3.a).

Spain's imperial legacy across Latin America directly connects to Spanish colonies in the Americas — discovery and conquest (B.1), colonial administration (B.2), Bourbon reforms (B.4), and North American expansion (B.5).
Locke's natural rights philosophy and government by consent directly influenced colonial cultural and scientific achievements and the intellectual foundations of American independence (C.3.c).
Elizabeth I's reign saw early English exploration and attempted settlement in North America through Drake and Raleigh (C.2).

The extension of European power over foreign territories through military and economic means directly frames the Spanish and Portuguese colonial systems (B), English colonies (C), and French settlements (D).
New York City's founding as Dutch Fort Amsterdam (~1624) and its becoming British territory connects to Dutch and Swedish settlement and later expulsion by the English (C.2).
The broad survey of human development including European exploration and colonization provides general context for the geography and ethnography of the Americas (A).
